1: The word comes to Jeremiah while he is confined in the court of the guard — the prophetic word is not silenced by imprisonment. Christ spoke from the ultimate confinement: the cross and the tomb were the world's attempt to silence the divine word, but the word that came while confined is the word that proved unkillable. Paul writes from prison (Philippians 1:12-14) that his confinement has advanced the gospel; the imprisoned prophet is a recurrent pattern for the unstoppable word of God.
2: The LORD identifies himself as the maker, former, and establisher of the earth — three creation verbs that ground the restoration promise in divine omnipotence. John's prologue applies this creator identity to Christ: 'All things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made' (John 1:3). The one who makes the restoration promise is the same one through whom the earth was originally made; the new creation is the work of the same creator-Word who spoke the first creation into being.
3: 'Call to me and I will answer you, and will tell you great and hidden things that you have not known.' Jesus reframes this as the heart of the new covenant's access: 'Ask and it will be given to you' (Matthew 7:7); 'Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do' (John 14:13). The 'great and hidden things' (bĕṣūrôt) — hidden, inaccessible truths — are what Paul calls 'the mystery hidden for ages' now revealed in Christ (Colossians 1:26-27): the indwelling of Christ in believers, the calling of the Gentiles, the fullness of the gospel.
4: Houses of city and kings torn down for siege ramps — destruction in judgment. The temple that stood as the false security of Jerusalem was torn down; Christ himself identified his body as the true temple (John 2:19-21), and the destruction of Herod's temple in 70 CE is predicted by Jesus as the consequence of rejecting him (Matthew 24:2; Luke 19:43-44). The rubble of Jerusalem's houses is the rubble of every human structure that substitutes for the presence of God incarnate.
5: 'I have hidden my face from this city because of all their wickedness.' The divine hiddenness — the withdrawal of the Shekinah — is the deepest judgment. On the cross Jesus cried 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46), entering the condition of God-forsakenness that Jerusalem experienced in the exile. Christ underwent the full weight of the hidden face of God — not merely as a metaphor but as the representative bearer of all the sin that caused the hiding.
6: 'Behold I will bring health and healing to it; I will heal them and reveal to them an abundance of prosperity and security.' Jesus opens his ministry quoting Isaiah 61 (Luke 4:18): 'He has sent me to proclaim good news to the poor, to bind up the brokenhearted.' Every healing in the Gospels is a concrete sign of this restoration promise: the healings are not merely compassionate acts but demonstrations that the kingdom of restoration — the shalom YHWH promised — has arrived in the person of the healer.
7: 'I will restore the fortunes of Judah and Israel and rebuild them as they were at the beginning.' Paul's vision in 2 Corinthians 5:17 is the Christological fulfillment: 'If anyone is in Christ he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold the new has come.' The rebuild 'as at the beginning' gestures toward the original Edenic purpose — which Christ restores not merely by returning to Eden but by surpassing it. The new creation in Christ is better than the original: Eden had no cross, no resurrection, no indwelling Spirit.
8: 'I will cleanse them from all the iniquity by which they sinned against me, and I will forgive all the iniquity and rebellion.' Hebrews applies this forgiveness promise directly to Christ's atonement: 'For by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified' (Hebrews 10:14); 'I will remember their sins and their lawless deeds no more' (Hebrews 10:17, citing Jeremiah 31:34). The forgiveness Jeremiah promises is not achieved by exile and return but by the definitive sacrifice of the Son — the blood that speaks better than Abel's (Hebrews 12:24).
9: 'This city will be to me a name of joy, a praise, and a glory before all the nations.' Philippians 2:9-11 transfers the honor-before-all-nations to Christ: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven and on earth.' Jerusalem's calling to be a praise before the nations is fulfilled eschatologically in the Lamb — Revelation pictures the nations bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:26), but the city's glory is the Lamb himself (Revelation 21:23).
10: 'In this place that you are calling a waste — without man or beast.' The silence of desolation is the silence of the grave: the ruined city is a figure of death. But into the silence of death comes the voice that says 'there shall again be heard ...' — the resurrection voice. Jesus's entry into the silence of death (the tomb) is the entry into the world's ultimate desolation; his resurrection is the voice that ends the silence and begins the new song.
11: 'The voice of joy and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride.' John the Baptist identifies himself as the friend of the bridegroom who rejoices when he hears the bridegroom's voice (John 3:29). Christ is the bridegroom; the church is the bride (Ephesians 5:25-32; Revelation 19:7-9). The eschatological wedding feast prophesied here is the marriage supper of the Lamb — the fullness of which the Lord's Supper is the advance foretaste: 'Give thanks to the LORD for his steadfast love endures forever' is the eucharistic cry of those who know the cross is that love's ultimate expression.
12: 'There will again be pastures where shepherds rest their flocks.' Jesus the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) makes the sheep lie down in green pastures (Psalms 23:2) — the rest imagery is consistently Christological. Hebrews 4:9-10 reads the rest that remains for the people of God through the lens of Christ's completed work: the one who has entered his rest has also rested from his works as God did from his. The desolate places will be places of rest because the Good Shepherd who laid down his life makes rest possible.
13: 'The flocks shall once more pass under the hands of the one who counts them.' The counter of the flock is Christ, who says 'I know my own and my own know me' (John 10:14) and 'I give them eternal life and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28-29). The numbering of the sheep is the security of election: every sheep that passes under the shepherd's hand is known, counted, and secured — nothing in all creation can separate them from the love of God in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:38-39).
14: 'The days are coming when I will fulfill the good promise I made to the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' Paul's great Christological affirmation: 'For all the promises of God find their Yes in him' (2 Corinthians 1:20). Every promise in the Hebrew scriptures — return from exile, restoration of fortunes, new covenant, righteous branch, eternal kingdom — finds its fulfillment not in the return under Zerubbabel (which was partial) but in Jesus Christ. The 'days are coming' of Jeremiah's prophecy have come in the person of the Son.
15: A direct messianic fulfillment: 'I will cause a righteous ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq (ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq) to spring up for David, and he shall execute justice and righteousness in the land.' Ṣemaḥ (Branch, Sprout) is the specific messianic title Zechariah uses twice (Zechariah 3:8; Jeremiah 6:12), and Revelation applies to Christ: 'the root and the offspring of David' (Revelation 5:5; Jeremiah 22:16). The Branch who springs up is not merely a king who does justice but the one who IS righteousness for his people — 1 Corinthians 1:30: 'Christ Jesus, whom God made our wisdom and our righteousness.' The righteous Branch is the one who justifies the ungodly (Romans 4:5).
16: 'And this is the name by which it will be called: YHWH ṣidqênû — The LORD is our righteousness.' Paul identifies this promise with Christ: 'Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes' (Romans 10:4); 'God made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). The name YHWH ṣidqênû, given to the restored Jerusalem, is the name of Christ's people because they are clothed in him — their righteousness is not their own but the Lord's, reckoned to them through faith in the Branch.
17: 'There will never fail to be a man from David's line to sit on the throne of the house of Israel.' The angel Gabriel applies this promise to Jesus at the annunciation: 'The Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever, and of his kingdom there will be no end' (Luke 1:32-33). Peter at Pentecost makes the connection explicit: David was a prophet and 'seeing what was ahead, he spoke of the resurrection of Christ' — the one who now sits on David's throne does so by virtue of the resurrection (Acts 2:29-31). The unbroken Davidic succession is Jesus.
18: 'The Levitical priests shall never lack a man in my presence to offer burnt offerings.' The royal Davidic covenant and the Levitical priestly covenant are here inseparably linked — both are as permanent as the cosmos. Hebrews resolves the puzzle of how Jesus, from the tribe of Judah, fulfills the Levitical promise: he is priest not by the Levitical order of physical descent but by the Melchizedekian order of indestructible life (Hebrews 7:14-17). He combines in himself both the royal Davidic promise (Jeremiah 33:17) and the perpetual priestly promise (Jeremiah 33:18) — king and priest in one person, the thing the law could not do but Christ has done.
19: The word of the LORD came to Jeremiah — the prophetic word is the pre-incarnate communication of the Son who is the eternal Word. John 1:1 establishes the Logos as the ground of all divine speech: every time 'the word of the LORD came,' it was the eternal Word — who would become flesh — who was speaking. The frequency of this formula in Jeremiah (over 50 times) marks the book as saturated with the pre-incarnate Christ's communicative activity.
20: 'If you can break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night, so that day and night no longer come at their appointed time.' The cosmic orders that sustain creation are the guarantors of the Davidic-priestly covenant. Paul's 'nothing in all creation will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 8:38-39) is the new covenant expression of the same logic: the love revealed in the cross is as indestructible as the cosmos — more so, because the cosmos itself will be renewed (Revelation 21:1) but Christ's love never changes.
21: 'Then my covenant with my servant David could also be broken, so that he would no longer have a son to reign on his throne.' The Davidic covenant's eternality is secured by the resurrection: the Son who reigns on David's throne is the one whom death could not hold (Acts 2:24). The resurrection is God's pledge that the covenant cannot be broken — 'the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant' (Hebrews 13:20). The risen Christ is the living proof that the Davidic covenant stands.
22: 'As the host of heaven cannot be numbered and the sand of the sea cannot be measured, so I will multiply the offspring of David my servant and the Levites who minister to me.' Galatians 3:16 focuses the seed promise: 'It does not say seeds, referring to many, but referring to one, and to your offspring, who is Christ.' All who are in Christ are Abraham's offspring and heirs of the promise (Galatians 3:29) — the multiplication happens through union with the one seed. The countless host of the redeemed in Revelation (Jeremiah 7:9: a great multitude that no one could number) is the fulfillment: the seed multiplied through Christ.
23: The word of the LORD came — the repeated formula of divine communication. Christ is the Word who speaks through the prophets (Hebrews 1:1) and who is himself the final, definitive speech (Hebrews 1:2: 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son'). Each prophetic word-reception is a type of the incarnation: God communicating to the human through a human vessel, until the vessel and the word become identical in the person of the Son.
24: 'The LORD has rejected the two families he chose' — the people's despairing conclusion. Romans 9-11 addresses precisely this question: has God rejected his people? Paul's answer is no — a remnant chosen by grace remains, and the hardening of Israel is partial and temporary, serving the purpose of Gentile inclusion (Romans 11:1-2, 11-12, 25-26). The apparent rejection of the two families during the exile and in the present age is not God's final word; the final word is the one who says 'I will restore their fortunes' (Jeremiah 33:26).
25: 'If I have not established my covenant with day and night and the fixed orders of heaven and earth.' Colossians 1:17 identifies Christ as the one in whom 'all things hold together' — the fixed orders of creation subsist in the Son's sustaining power (Hebrews 1:3: he upholds the universe by the word of his power). The cosmic-covenant logic runs both ways: the Creator who sustains the cosmos is the same one who sustains the covenant; both rest on the faithfulness of Christ.
26: 'I will restore their fortunes and have mercy on them.' The divine 'I will' of covenantal mercy is the heartbeat of Paul's argument in Romans 11:26-27: 'The deliverer will come from Zion, he will banish ungodliness from Jacob; and this will be my covenant with them when I take away their sins.' The restoration of Israel's fortunes is eschatologically achieved through Christ — the deliverer who has come and will come again. All mercy flows through the Son who embodies it (Luke 1:78: the tender mercy of our God).
